tools.name: Zuddl
tools.one_liner: Zuddl is a unified event platform that pairs registration and ticketing with check-in, an attendee app, and CRM sync in one system.
strengths: Drag-and-drop flow builder for multi-step, conditional registration without developer help.
Branded landing pages and embeddable widgets that keep the experience on the organizer's own domain.
Native, bi-directional sync with Salesforce, HubSpot, and Marketo so registrations and attendance update CRM records in real time.
Tiered ticketing with complex discounting, promo codes, and approval flows for invite-only audiences.
Registration connects directly to on-site check-in, badge printing, and the attendee app from the same platform.
gaps: The breadth of the unified platform can create a learning curve during initial setup, though all plans include onboarding and first-event support.
tools.best_for: B2B teams that want registration, ticketing, check-in, and CRM data on one platform instead of stitched across point tools.

tools.name: Cvent
tools.one_liner: Cvent is an enterprise event management suite with a deep registration feature set built for large, complex programs.
strengths: Granular registration paths with session capacity rules, waitlists, and complex approval logic.
Mature reporting across the full event lifecycle within one vendor ecosystem.
Large integration catalog covering major CRMs and marketing automation tools.
gaps: Setup is heavy: reviewers report months of configuration and sometimes external consultants.
Interface and registration pages feel dated compared with newer platforms.
Total cost runs high once add-on modules are included.
tools.best_for: Enterprises with an existing Cvent contract running a high volume of complex, multi-session conferences.

tools.name: Swoogo
tools.one_liner: Swoogo is a registration-first platform known for flexible forms and pages without per-registration fees.
strengths: Highly customizable registration forms and landing pages with conditional logic.
Flat annual pricing with unlimited events and registrations rather than per-ticket fees.
Strong reporting and a clean admin experience that teams set up without developers.
gaps: Not a full virtual or attendee-app platform; on-site features are lighter.
Some integrations require setup or middleware to reach real-time CRM sync.
tools.best_for: Mid-market teams that want deep registration customization and predictable pricing without enterprise overhead.

tools.name: Eventbrite
tools.one_liner: Eventbrite is a fast-to-launch ticketing platform with built-in payments and an audience discovery marketplace.
tools.pricing: Free for free events; paid tiers from a per-ticket fee
strengths: Quick setup and built-in payment processing for paid tickets.
Public discovery marketplace can drive incremental registrations.
Familiar attendee checkout that needs little explanation.
gaps: Limited branding and no true white-label registration experience.
Light CRM and marketing integrations relative to B2B-focused tools.
Not built for complex, multi-track or invite-only B2B registration.
tools.best_for: Smaller or community-style events and B2C-leaning programs where speed and reach beat deep customization.

tools.name: Splash
tools.one_liner: Splash focuses on beautifully branded event pages and email for distributed marketing teams running many events.
strengths: On-brand registration pages and templates that scale across many events and teams.
Good marketing-stack integrations for email and attribution.
Approval and brand-control features suited to distributed field-marketing teams.
gaps: Lighter on complex ticketing and payment scenarios.
Reporting depth trails dedicated registration or enterprise platforms.
tools.best_for: Brand-led field marketing teams running a high volume of smaller events that must stay on-brand.

tools.name: Bizzabo
tools.one_liner: Bizzabo is an event experience platform that unifies the event website, registration, and attendee app.
strengths: Shared content backend keeps the event website and registration in sync.
Klik wearable integration captures on-site engagement data.
Solid analytics across registration and attendance.
gaps: Customization beyond basic branding is limited for some page types.
Iframe-based pages can create tracking gaps for GA4 or attribution tools.
Pricing sits at the higher end for mid-market budgets.
tools.best_for: Marketing teams running branded user conferences that want website, registration, and app unified.

dimensions.name: registration flow flexibility
dimensions.why: Without conditional, multi-path forms you end up with manual workarounds for VIPs, sessions, and approvals.

dimensions.name: branding and white-labeling
dimensions.why: A generic, vendor-branded page lowers conversion and weakens brand trust before the event even starts.

dimensions.name: ticketing and payments
dimensions.why: Tiered pricing, discounts, and reliable payment processing decide whether finance trusts the numbers.

dimensions.name: CRM and marketing sync
dimensions.why: Delayed or one-way sync means sales and marketing chase stale lists after the event.

dimensions.name: approval and invite workflows
dimensions.why: Curated audiences need approval gates, or you cannot run invite-only or partner tracks cleanly.

dimensions.name: on-site check-in integration
dimensions.why: If registration does not feed check-in and badges, you re-enter data and queues build at the door.

dimensions.name: reporting and analytics
dimensions.why: Registration, revenue, and attendance numbers should export cleanly, or you rebuild reports by hand.

dimensions.name: scalability and reliability
dimensions.why: High-volume on-sales and global events expose platforms that buckle under load or lack localization.
